title: Animated Plots with Plotly
text: Plotly charts are fully interactive — zoom, pan, hover for values — and animations add a time dimension with a built-in scrubber.

title: Animated Plots with Plotly
text:
Lissajous figure
A Lissajous curve traces \(x = \sin(at + \delta),\ y = \sin(bt)\). Animating over the phase offset \(\delta\) shows how the shape morphs as the two oscillations drift in and out of phase.

import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go

t = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 500)
deltas = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 60)

frames = [
    go.Frame(
        data=[go.Scatter(
            x=np.sin(2 * t + d),
            y=np.sin(3 * t),
            mode="lines",
            line=dict(color="steelblue", width=2),
        )],
        name=str(i),
    )
    for i, d in enumerate(deltas)
]

fig = go.Figure(
    data=frames[0].data,
    frames=frames,
    layout=go.Layout(
        xaxis=dict(range=[-1.1, 1.1], zeroline=False, title="x"),
        yaxis=dict(range=[-1.1, 1.1], zeroline=False, title="y", scaleanchor="x"),
        width=500,
        height=500,
        updatemenus=[dict(
            type="buttons",
            showactive=False,
            buttons=[
                dict(label="Play",  method="animate",
                     args=[None, {"frame": {"duration": 50}, "fromcurrent": True}]),
                dict(label="Pause", method="animate",
                     args=[[None], {"frame": {"duration": 0}, "mode": "immediate"}]),
            ],
        )],
        sliders=[dict(
            steps=[dict(method="animate", args=[[f.name]], label="") for f in frames],
            transition=dict(duration=0),
            x=0, y=0, len=1.0,
        )],
    ),
)

fig.show()

title: Animated Plots with Plotly
text:
Spiral build-up
A simpler example: a spiral that draws itself point by point.

theta = np.linspace(0, 4 * np.pi, 200)
r = theta / (4 * np.pi)
x = r * np.cos(theta)
y = r * np.sin(theta)

frames = [
    go.Frame(data=[go.Scatter(x=x[:k], y=y[:k], mode="lines",
                              line=dict(color="coral", width=2))], name=str(k))
    for k in range(2, len(x) + 1, 2)
]

fig2 = go.Figure(
    data=frames[0].data,
    frames=frames,
    layout=go.Layout(
        xaxis=dict(range=[-1.1, 1.1], zeroline=False, showticklabels=False),
        yaxis=dict(range=[-1.1, 1.1], zeroline=False, showticklabels=False,
                   scaleanchor="x"),
        width=500, height=500,
        updatemenus=[dict(
            type="buttons", showactive=False,
            buttons=[
                dict(label="Play",  method="animate",
                     args=[None, {"frame": {"duration": 20}, "fromcurrent": True}]),
                dict(label="Pause", method="animate",
                     args=[[None], {"frame": {"duration": 0}, "mode": "immediate"}]),
            ],
        )],
    ),
)

fig2.show()

title: Post With Code
text:
A noisy sine wave


Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
t = np.linspace(0, 4 * np.pi, 300)
signal = np.sin(t)
noisy = signal + rng.normal(0, 0.3, size=t.size)



fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 3))
ax.plot(t, noisy, color="0.7", label="noisy")
ax.plot(t, signal, color="black", label="true")
ax.legend()
plt.show()







Figure 1: Noisy signal (grey) vs. true sine (black).





As shown in Figure 1, the noise is roughly symmetric around the true signal.
